items.title: Understanding the Causative Agent and Its Identification
items.content: Donovanosis is caused by Klebsiella granulomatis, although there's debate about its classification. The organism was first identified in 1905 with characteristic Donovan bodies measuring $1.5 \times 0.7$ $\mu$m found in macrophages and the stratum malpighii.
keyPoints: First described in Calcutta, identified by Charles Donovan in Madras

items.title: Geographic Distribution and Socioeconomic Factors of Donovanosis
items.content: Donovanosis has a unique geographic distribution with historical prevalence in regions like Papua New Guinea, southern Africa, India, the Caribbean, French Guyana, Brazil, and Aboriginal communities in Australia. It is associated with poor hygiene, more common in lower socioeconomic groups, and predominantly affects men. The incidence has decreased globally due to better management of genital ulcers.
keyPoints: Geographic distribution: Papua New Guinea, southern Africa, India, Caribbean, French Guyana, Brazil, Aboriginal communities in Australia
Historical prevalence: 5000–10,000 cases in the US (1947), largest epidemic in Dutch South Guinea (1922-1952)
Socioeconomic factors: More common in lower socioeconomic groups and men
Global incidence decline due to better management of genital ulcers

items.title: Recognizing Donovanosis Lesions for Early Diagnosis
items.content: Donovanosis lesions begin as papules or nodules that ulcerate, with four distinct types: beefy red ulcers, hypertrophic ulcers, necrotic ulcers, and sclerotic lesions. Genital involvement is common (90%), often mimicking other conditions like cervical carcinoma.
keyPoints: Lesions start as papules or nodules that ulcerate after trauma.
Four types of lesions: beefy red ulcer, hypertrophic/verrucous ulcer, necrotic ulcer, sclerotic lesion.
Genital involvement in 90% of patients; cervical lesions may mimic carcinoma.

items.title: Diagnosing Donovanosis: Microscopic Identification and PCR Testing
items.content: Donovanosis diagnosis is confirmed by identifying Donovan bodies in tissue smears. A smear should be taken before other tests to ensure material collection. PCR testing differentiates K. granulomatis from related species, aiding in accurate diagnosis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis confirmed by microscopic identification of Donovan bodies.
Smear preparation is crucial; take before swab samples for other causes.
PCR test based on unique base changes in the phoE gene.

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Donovanosis
items.content: Donovanosis must be differentiated from conditions like syphilis, chancroid, and genital herpes. Mixed infections are common, necessitating careful histologic examination to distinguish from other diseases such as rhinoscleroma and leishmaniasis.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes syphilitic chancres, chancroid, lymphogranuloma venereum, genital herpes, neoplasm, amebiasis.
Histologic differentiation from rhinoscleroma, leishmaniasis, and histoplasmosis.

items.question: What is the current name for the causative organism of Donovanosis based on phylogenetic analysis?
items.explanation: The causative organism has been reclassified as Klebsiella granulomatis comb nov based on phylogenetic analysis.

items.question: Where was Donovanosis first described?
items.explanation: Donovanosis was first described in Calcutta in 1882.

items.question: In which country has Donovanosis been almost entirely eliminated through a sustained health program?
items.explanation: In Australia, donovanosis has been almost entirely eliminated through a sustained program backed by strong political commitment and resources.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of lesion associated with donovanosis?
options: Fibrous and scar tissue without ulceration
items.explanation: The sclerotic or cicatricial lesion involves fibrous and scar tissue but is not categorized as a type of lesion that lacks ulceration.

items.question: Which site is most commonly affected by donovanosis in men?
items.explanation: The coronal sulcus is one of the most common sites of infection in men.

items.question: What is a key diagnostic feature of donovanosis when examining tissue smears?
items.explanation: Donovan bodies, which are gram-negative intracytoplasmic cysts, are a key diagnostic feature.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the differential diagnosis for donovanosis?
items.explanation: Tuberculosis is not listed as part of the differential diagnosis for donovanosis.

items.statement: The organism causing Donovanosis was reproducibly cultured in the early 20th century.
items.explanation: The organism was not reproducibly cultured until the mid-1990s.

items.statement: Donovanosis is a risk factor for HIV infection.
items.explanation: Donovanosis is associated with an increased risk of HIV infection.

items.statement: Donovanosis lesions are commonly associated with lymphadenitis.
Serologic tests are currently the preferred method for diagnosing donovanosis.
items.explanation: Serologic tests are poorly specific and not used for diagnosis.

items.assertion: Donovanosis can lead to neoplastic changes.
items.reason: Complications of donovanosis include neoplastic changes, pseudoelephantiasis, and stenosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Donovan bodies can be identified using a rapid Giemsa or Wright's stain.
items.reason: Direct microscopy with these stains allows for the visualization of gram-negative intracytoplasmic cysts filled with deeply staining bodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
